title: The role of GABA in calming an overactive threat detection system
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The chemical your nervous system is starving for 

 Your threat detection system is not broken. It is doing exactly what it was designed to do. The problem is...

The chemical your nervous system is starving for 

 Your threat detection system is not broken. It is doing exactly what it was designed to do. The proble...
htmlContent:
The chemical your nervous system is starving for

Your threat detection system is not broken. It is doing exactly what it was designed to do. The problem is that it never got the memo that the danger is over.

I spent years trying to talk myself out of being scared. I used logic. I used affirmations. I used every cognitive strategy in the book. And none of it worked. Because my nervous system was not listening to my thoughts. It was listening to a chemical imbalance I did not even know I had.

Here's the thing. Your brain has a brake system. And that brake system runs on a neurotransmitter called GABA. Gamma-aminobutyric acid. It is the main inhibitory neurotransmitter in your central nervous system. When GABA is working properly, it tells your neurons to calm down. It says, "We're safe now. You can stop firing." Without enough GABA, your threat detection system stays stuck in the on position. You are constantly scanning for danger. Your body is always bracing for impact. And you cannot think your way out of that.

Let me say that again because it matters. You cannot think your way out of a nervous system that lacks the chemical resources to calm down.

What happens when GABA is low

When GABA levels are insufficient, your brain's excitatory neurotransmitters like glutamate run unchecked. Think of it like a car with a stuck accelerator and no brakes. Glutamate keeps the engine revving. GABA is supposed to tell it to slow down. When the balance tips, you get stuck in a state of hyperarousal. Your threat detection system is scanning constantly. You startle easily. Your muscles stay tense. Your sleep is light and unrefreshing. You might feel wired but exhausted at the same time.

This is where trauma survivors often get stuck. Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma gets trapped in the body. He talks about the incomplete defensive responses that keep the nervous system locked in fight or flight. What he does not always say explicitly is that this locked state has a biochemical component. Your body cannot complete the stress response cycle if it does not have the chemical tools to downregulate afterward.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), showed us that trauma is not just a memory stored in your mind. It is a physiological reality stored in your body. Your nervous system learns to expect danger. It primes itself for threat. And over time, this becomes your baseline. You do not even realize you are in a state of chronic hypervigilance because it has become your normal.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office describing my anxiety as a low hum that never stopped. She asked me, "When was the last time you felt truly calm?" I could not remember. That is what low GABA feels like. It is not always panic attacks. Sometimes it is just a constant background noise of unease that you have learned to live with.

How GABA actually works in your brain

GABA binds to receptors on your neurons and makes them less likely to fire. It increases the threshold for activation. In simple terms, it raises the bar for what your nervous system considers worth reacting to. When GABA is working well, you can hear a loud noise and your body will orient briefly, assess, and then return to baseline. When GABA is low, that same noise triggers a full threat response that takes hours to settle.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains this through the lens of the vagus nerve. The ventral vagal pathway is what allows us to feel safe and connected. It is the brake on our sympathetic nervous system. And GABA plays a direct role in supporting that ventral vagal state. Without enough GABA, your nervous system cannot access the social engagement system that helps you feel calm in connection with others.

Think about what happens when you drink alcohol. Alcohol works partly by enhancing GABA activity. That is why it feels relaxing at first. Your threat detection system quiets down. Your social anxiety drops. You feel more present and less guarded. But alcohol is a blunt instrument. It floods your system with GABAergic activity and then leaves you with a rebound effect. The next day, your GABA levels are even lower than before. This is why alcohol is not a sustainable solution for nervous system regulation. It borrows calm from tomorrow and charges interest.

Stay with me here. There are other ways to support GABA function that do not come with the same cost. Things like magnesium, taurine, and L-theanine all influence GABA activity. They are gentler. They work with your body's natural rhythms instead of overwhelming them.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). L-theanine is an amino acid found in green tea that increases GABA levels without causing drowsiness. It promotes a state of calm alertness. I have found it helpful for those moments when my nervous system is buzzing but I still need to function.

What actually raises GABA naturally

Your body produces GABA from glutamate using an enzyme called glutamic acid decarboxylase. This process requires vitamin B6 as a cofactor. If you are deficient in B6, your body cannot make enough GABA. This is one reason why chronic stress depletes your nutrient stores. Stress uses up B vitamins. Low B vitamins means less GABA. Less GABA means more stress. It is a vicious cycle.

There are also lifestyle practices that support GABA production. Yoga has been shown to increase GABA levels. One study found that a single 60-minute yoga session increased GABA by 27 percent compared to reading. This is not about flexibility or fitness. It is about the specific combination of movement, breath, and focused attention that signals safety to your nervous system.

Breath work matters too. Slow, extended exhalations stimulate the vagus nerve and promote GABA release. The physiological sigh, two inhales through the nose followed by a long exhale through the mouth, is particularly effective. It is not woo-woo. It is basic neurobiology.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn. He explores how trauma can be passed down through generations and how that shows up in your nervous system. Understanding the source of your hypervigilance can help you stop blaming yourself for a response that was never your fault.

Sleep is another critical factor. GABA levels naturally rise during the early stages of sleep and decline toward morning. If you are not sleeping well, your GABA cycle gets disrupted. This creates a feedback loop where poor sleep lowers GABA and low GABA makes it harder to sleep. Breaking that loop often requires addressing both sides at once.

Exercise also influences GABA. Moderate intensity aerobic activity increases GABA levels in the brain. But here is the catch. High intensity exercise can actually increase cortisol and glutamate, making things worse for someone with an already overactive threat detection system. The key is finding the right dose. For many trauma survivors, gentle movement like walking or swimming works better than high intensity training.

When GABA alone is not enough

I need to be honest with you about something. GABA supplements exist, but they do not cross the blood-brain barrier effectively. Taking GABA orally does not reliably increase GABA levels in your brain. The supplements that work are precursors and cofactors. Things like L-theanine, magnesium, taurine, and B6. They give your body the raw materials it needs to produce its own GABA.

There is also the question of GABA receptors. Chronic stress and trauma can downregulate your GABA receptors. Your brain has fewer places for GABA to bind. This is called receptor desensitization. It means you need more GABA to achieve the same calming effect. This is one reason why trauma survivors often need higher doses of calming supplements or medications to feel any effect at all.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. He explores how chronic stress and suppressed emotions contribute to physical disease. The connection between your threat detection system and your overall health is not metaphorical. It is biochemical. Your nervous system is literally shaping your physiology every moment of every day.

Recovery is not about eliminating the threat response. It is about restoring the balance between excitation and inhibition. It is about giving your nervous system the resources it needs to come back to baseline after a threat has passed. This takes time. It takes consistency. And it takes a willingness to work with your biology instead of against it.

Wild, right? The thing you have been fighting, the constant vigilance and hyperarousal, might not be a character flaw. It might be a chemical imbalance that your body is doing its best to manage with the resources it has.

I cannot tell you that supporting GABA will fix everything. It will not. Trauma recovery is more complex than any single neurotransmitter. But I can tell you that ignoring the biochemical side of your nervous system regulation is like trying to fix a car with a dead battery by polishing the paint. It looks better on the surface, but it still will not start.

Your threat detection system is not your enemy. It is trying to protect you with the tools it has. Give it better tools. Feed it what it needs. And maybe, over time, it will learn that not every sound is a predator and not every shadow is a threat.

Sit with that.
